summary: Hellenistic philosophical school founded by Epicurus, arguing that pleasure (especially the absence of pain) is the highest good. The Epicureans advocated atomism, the clinamen (swerve), ataraxia (freedom from distress), aponia (freedom from pain), and the tetrapharmakos (fourfold remedy). Lucretius popularized the school in De Rerum Natura. The school taught at 'the Garden.'

works.name: Atomism and the Clinamen (Swerve)
works.description: Epicurean physics is grounded in atomism inherited from Democritus. The key innovation is the clinamen or 'swerve' -- atoms falling through the void spontaneously deviate, creating randomness and explaining free will. Lucretius expounded this in the six-book poem De Rerum Natura, which Stephen Greenblatt titled his book The Swerve after. The Epicureans believed the gods occupied areas devoid of atoms called metakosmia. All sense perception is considered true, illustrated by the example of a square tower appearing round from a distance.

clues.clue: Clinamen/swerve: atoms spontaneously deviate, creating randomness and free will
examples: free will exists because atoms falling through the cosmos experience clinamen, or swerve
the 'swerve' to explain how randomness arises in an atomistic world

examples: Stephen Greenblatt's book The Swerve
A Stephen Greenblatt book about the birth of Renaissance culture

examples: the gods occupied an area devoid of atoms called the metakosmia

works.name: Ataraxia, Aponia, and the Tetrapharmakos
works.description: The goal of Epicurean life is ataraxia (freedom from distress/disturbance) and aponia (absence of pain). The tetrapharmakos ('fourfold remedy') is a daily recitation from the 'principal doctrines' arguing: don't fear gods, don't fear death, what is good is easy to get, what is terrible is easy to endure. The founder's argument against fearing death: 'when we are, death is not come, and when death is come, we are not.' Gravestones bore 'Non fui, fui, non sum' ('I was not, I was, I am not'). Followers celebrated the holiday Eikas on the twentieth of every month.

examples: the highest good was ataraxia, the absence of pain
freedom from distress and freedom from pain, known as ataraxia and aponia

works.name: The Garden and Epicurean Community
works.description: Epicurus taught at a school called 'the Garden,' whose gate inscription reportedly read 'Stranger, here you will do well to tarry.' He enjoyed a simple diet of barley bread and water. In Naples, Philodemus of Gadara led a circle of Epicureans as a client of Lucius Calpurnius Piso. Diogenes of Oenoanda carved Epicurean doctrines on a portico wall. The school was opposed to both Stoicism and Platonism; Plutarch wrote that it is impossible to live well according to Epicureanism. Pierre Gassendi led an early-modern revival.

works.name: Problem of Evil and Reception
works.description: Epicurus is credited with introducing the problem of evil (his 'namesake paradox'), popularized in David Hume's Dialogues Concerning Natural Religion as a trilemma. The school's materialist, atomist worldview was compared to the Indian Carvaka school. In modern reception, Tolstoy discussed adopting Epicureanism in A Confession, and Ninon de Lenclos is considered a key figure in the 17th-century revival. Santayana's Three Philosophical Poets begins with Lucretius as an Epicurean.

clues.clue: Problem of evil: Epicurus's namesake paradox; popularized in Hume's Dialogues
examples: credited with introducing the problem of evil in David Hume's Dialogues Concerning Natural Religion

examples: The Carvakas have been compared to this Greek thinker's lineage

clues.clue: Tolstoy: one way of handling hopelessness in A Confession
examples: In A Confession, one way Tolstoy sees of handling the hopelessness of life is to adopt this Ancient Greek school of thought

clues.clue: Santayana's Three Philosophical Poets begins with Lucretius
examples: The first of Santayana's Three Philosophical Poets is Lucretius

comprehensive_summary:
Epicureanism is a Hellenistic philosophical school founded by Epicurus, centered on the pursuit of pleasure understood primarily as the absence of pain. The school's physics derives from Democritus's atomism, with the crucial innovation of the clinamen or 'swerve' -- atoms spontaneously deviating from their path, which explains both randomness and free will. Lucretius's six-book poem De Rerum Natura is the primary surviving text expounding Epicurean physics, and Stephen Greenblatt's The Swerve discusses its rediscovery.

The ethical goal is achieving ataraxia (freedom from distress) and aponia (absence of pain). The tetrapharmakos ('fourfold remedy'), drawn from the 'principal doctrines,' was recited daily. Epicurus argued that fearing death is irrational ('when we are, death is not come'). Followers' gravestones bore 'Non fui, fui, non sum.' The school celebrated the holiday Eikas on the twentieth of each month.

Epicurus taught at 'the Garden' in Athens, living simply on barley bread and water. He argued that all sense perception is true, using the example of a square tower appearing round. He posited that gods occupy the metakosmia (spaces between atoms) and have no concern for mortals. The school established three criteria of truth: feelings, preconceptions, and sense-perceptions.

Philodemus of Gadara led a circle in Naples (writings found at Herculaneum). Diogenes of Oenoanda carved doctrines on a wall. The school opposed Stoicism and Platonism. Epicurus is credited with the problem of evil, popularized through Hume. Pierre Gassendi revived the school in the early modern period. The Epicureans were compared to India's Carvaka school for shared hedonistic and atomistic doctrines.
recursive_suggestions: Lucretius / De Rerum Natura (heavily clued poem)

cards.front: Concept: atoms spontaneously deviate from their path, creating randomness and explaining free will in Epicurean and Lucretian physics
Philosopher: used the example of a square tower appearing round to argue that all sense perception is true, but judgment of perception can be fallible
Philosopher: conjectured that the gods occupy areas devoid of atoms called metakosmia and have no concern for mortals
Concept: daily Epicurean recitation from the 'principal doctrines,' a fourfold remedy arguing against fear of gods and death
Concept: Epicurean inscription on gravestones translating to 'I was not, I was, I am not, I do not care'
cards.back: Non fui, fui, non sum, non curo

cards.front: Philosopher: led a circle of Epicureans in Naples whose writings were found in the Villa of the Papyri at Herculaneum
Philosopher: taught at a school called 'the Garden,' whose gate reportedly read 'Stranger, here you will do well to tarry'
Philosopher: established three criteria of truth: feelings, preconceptions (prolepseis), and sense-perceptions
Philosopher: credited with introducing the problem of evil, popularized as a trilemma in David Hume's Dialogues Concerning Natural Religion
